enrichment.short_description: Rotato is a Mac app that turns app screenshots and screen recordings into photorealistic 3D device mockup videos and images for product marketing.
enrichment.pricing_model: One-time lifetime license purchase across three tiers, plus a watermarked free trial (no ongoing subscription)
enrichment.company_size: Small, self-funded indie team; bootstrapped and profitable with no outside investors
enrichment.seo_title: Rotato Review 2026: Pricing, Features and 3D Mockup Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Rotato costs, which 3D device mockup and animation features it offers, and whether this Mac-only lifetime-license tool fits your product marketing workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rotato is a macOS application founded in 2019 by Morten, a former Google in-house incubator employee, that lets designers, marketers, and indie app makers turn plain screenshots or screen recordings into photorealistic 3D device mockup videos and images without needing skills in Cinema 4D, Blender, or After Effects. The company is based in Zurich, Switzerland, is self-funded and profitable, and has never raised outside investment, reporting over 150,000 installs in its own marketing materials. The core workflow lets a user drag a screenshot into a 3D scene, choose from more than 30 device mockups covering iPhone, iPad, MacBook, and Android hardware, position the device in real-time 3D space, adjust lighting and background, and set keyframes so the camera and screen content animate automatically between states such as rotating, floating, or zooming. Output formats include MP4, MOV, GIF, and static PNG, with resolution up to 4K on the Standard plan and 8K on the Premium plan; Premium additionally supports ProRes4444 with alpha-channel export for compositing in Premiere or Final Cut, plus HEVC/H.265. Rotato is sold as a one-time, lifetime-license purchase rather than a subscription, split into three tiers: Basic at $79 (phone mockups only, six months of free updates), Standard at $89 (the full 30-plus device library, one gallery template, 18 premium video templates, 12 months of updates, 4K export), and Premium at $199 (100 premium video templates, 50-plus gallery templates, two website templates, 8K export, a Figma plugin, clay-render mode, and camera lens controls, also with 12 months of updates). There is no permanent free tier, but a watermarked free trial is available before purchase. Rotato is Mac-only, with no native Windows or web version, which is the most consistently cited limitation in reviews. It is popular among SaaS founders and indie developers producing Product Hunt launch assets, App Store and Google Play preview videos, and landing-page hero visuals, as well as among design agencies and freelancers building UX presentation reels for clients. Competitors frequently compared to Rotato include Screen Studio and Matte, both of which use subscription pricing rather than Rotato's one-time-purchase model, a distinction Rotato emphasizes heavily as a selling point for buyers who prefer to avoid recurring software costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rotato is a Mac-only application for creating 3D device mockups and animated product videos from ordinary screenshots or screen recordings. Founded in 2019 by former Google incubator employee Morten and based in Zurich, Switzerland, the company is self-funded, has never taken outside investment, and remains profitable while serving a global base of indie makers, marketers, and design agencies.

Instead of requiring specialized 3D software like Cinema 4D or After Effects, Rotato provides a real-time 3D engine with pre-built device models and animation presets, letting non-designers produce studio-quality mockup videos in minutes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rotato ships with more than 30 photorealistic device mockups spanning iPhone, iPad, MacBook, and Android hardware, plus a library of prebuilt video and image templates that can be customized by dragging in a screenshot or screen recording. Users can freely rotate devices in 3D space, adjust lighting and backgrounds, and set camera keyframes for automatic rotate, float, or zoom animations.

Export options scale by plan, from standard H.264 video up to 4K and 8K resolution, with Premium adding ProRes4444 (including alpha channel) and HEVC/H.265 for professional post-production pipelines, along with a Figma plugin and clay-render mode.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Rotato uses a one-time lifetime-license model rather than a subscription. The Basic tier costs $79 and covers phone mockups only with six months of included updates; Standard costs $89 and unlocks the full 30-plus device library with 12 months of updates and 4K export; Premium costs $199 and adds the full template library, 8K export, ProRes4444/HEVC, the Figma plugin, and clay mode.
There is no permanent free plan, but prospective buyers can try Rotato via a watermarked free trial before purchasing a license.

features.title: 30-plus 3D device mockups
features.description: A library covering iPhone, iPad, MacBook, and Android devices that screenshots or screen recordings can be dropped into and positioned in real-time 3D space.

features.title: Animated camera keyframes
features.description: Users set keyframes for camera position and on-screen content, and Rotato interpolates smooth rotate, float, or zoom animations automatically.

features.title: High-resolution export
features.description: Video and image export up to 4K (Standard) or 8K (Premium), plus GIF and static PNG output for marketing and app store use.

features.title: ProRes4444 and HEVC export
features.description: Premium-tier export formats with alpha-channel support for compositing device footage into Premiere, Final Cut, or After Effects projects.

features.title: Figma plugin
features.description: A Premium-tier plugin that lets designers pull screens directly from Figma into a Rotato 3D scene.

features.title: Clay render mode and lens controls
features.description: Premium-only stylistic rendering and camera lens adjustments for more cinematic or abstract mockup shots.

features.title: Prebuilt template gallery
features.description: Dozens of ready-made video and image templates that can be restyled with a brand's own screenshots rather than built from scratch.

features.title: One-time lifetime licensing
features.description: All plans are sold as a single upfront payment rather than a recurring subscription, with a fixed window of included updates.

pros: No recurring subscription fee; pricing is a single one-time payment per tier
Produces photorealistic 3D mockup videos without 3D design skills or software like Cinema 4D
Large built-in library of devices and prebuilt templates speeds up production
Supports professional export formats (ProRes4444, HEVC) for editing pipelines
Self-funded, actively maintained product with a large existing install base
cons: Mac-only; no Windows or browser-based version is available
No permanent free tier, only a watermarked trial
Full device library, templates, and 8K export require the higher-priced tiers
Advanced camera and animation customization has a learning curve for first-time users

faqs.question: Is Rotato free to use?
faqs.answer: Rotato has no permanent free tier. It offers a watermarked free trial so users can test the app before purchasing one of its one-time-payment license tiers, which start at $79.

faqs.question: Does Rotato work on Windows?
faqs.answer: No. Rotato is built exclusively for macOS and has no native Windows or web-based version.

faqs.question: Is Rotato a subscription?
faqs.answer: No. Rotato is sold as a one-time lifetime-license purchase across three tiers (Basic, Standard, Premium), each including a fixed window of free updates rather than ongoing monthly billing.

faqs.question: What export formats does Rotato support?
faqs.answer: Rotato exports MP4, MOV, GIF, and static PNG files, with resolution up to 4K on Standard and 8K on Premium; Premium also adds ProRes4444 with alpha channel and HEVC/H.265.

faqs.question: How many device mockups does Rotato include?
faqs.answer: The Standard and Premium tiers include more than 30 device mockups spanning iPhone, iPad, MacBook, and Android hardware; the Basic tier is limited to phone mockups only.

faqs.question: Does Rotato integrate with Figma?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only on the Premium plan, which includes a dedicated Figma plugin for pulling screens directly into a 3D scene.

faqs.question: Who makes Rotato?
faqs.answer: Rotato was founded in 2019 by Morten, a former Google in-house incubator employee, and is a self-funded, profitable company based in Zurich, Switzerland.

faqs.question: Who is Rotato best suited for?
faqs.answer: Rotato is aimed at SaaS founders, indie app developers, and design agencies who need App Store previews, Product Hunt launch visuals, or client UX presentation videos without hiring a 3D motion designer.

use_cases.title: Product launch marketing visuals
use_cases.description: Indie makers and SaaS founders use Rotato to create polished device mockup videos and images for Product Hunt launches, landing pages, and social posts.

use_cases.title: App store preview videos
use_cases.description: Mobile teams use Rotato to turn screen recordings into App Store and Google Play preview videos featuring realistic device frames.

use_cases.title: Client UX presentation decks
use_cases.description: Design agencies and freelancers use Rotato's animation presets and templates to deliver polished mockup reels for client pitches and case studies.

features: Figma plugin, clay mode, and camera lens control

enrichment.short_description: Roundcube is a free, open-source AJAX-based webmail client written in PHP that gives IMAP email a desktop-like browser interface.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (GNU GPL v3 or later); no official paid tiers, though hosting providers may bundle or manage it as part of paid hosting
enrichment.seo_title: Roundcube Webmail: Free Open-Source IMAP Email Client Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Roundcube webmail is, its open-source history since 2008, core IMAP features, plugin ecosystem, and why it is free to self-host on any server.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Roundcube is a free and open-source, AJAX-based webmail client written in PHP that provides a desktop-like interface for reading and managing IMAP email accounts directly in a web browser. Development began in the mid-2000s under founder Thomas Bruederli, and the first stable release, version 0.1, was announced on March 4, 2008, after roughly two years of development; the project has been maintained continuously since. Roundcube is licensed under the GNU GPL version 3 or later, with exceptions that allow proprietary skins and plugins, and the codebase incorporates other open-source components such as the TinyMCE rich text editor, the Googiespell spell-checking library, and an HTML5-PHP sanitizer. It runs on a standard LAMP or LEMP stack (Linux, Apache or Nginx, MySQL/MariaDB or PostgreSQL, PHP) and requires no proprietary infrastructure, which is why it became a default bundled webmail option for many shared-hosting control panels; cPanel adopted Roundcube as its bundled webmail client, replacing SquirrelMail, and it remains commonly bundled with Plesk and DirectAdmin as well. In 2015, Roundcube and Kolab Systems AG announced plans for a full rewrite called Roundcube Next, backed by a crowdfunding campaign that raised US$103,541 against an $80,000 goal, but that rewrite effort was halted in 2016 and the original Roundcube codebase continued as the actively developed project. Since 2023, the project's fiscal and organizational home has been hosted under Nextcloud, which supports ongoing development and infrastructure. Core features include full IMAP support, an integrated address book, folder and message management, message search, spell checking, and MIME handling for attachments, and the interface is skinnable and extendable through a documented plugin API that lets administrators add features like two-factor authentication, calendaring (via plugins such as the Kolab-affiliated calendar plugin), or custom branding. Because it is free, self-hosted, and has no vendor lock-in, Roundcube is widely used by web hosts, universities, ISPs, and privacy-conscious organizations that want to run their own webmail front end on top of an existing IMAP/SMTP mail server rather than paying for a commercial hosted email suite. It has no company behind it in the traditional SaaS sense, no headquarters, and no per-seat pricing; costs to an organization are limited to hosting the software and optional support arrangements with third parties, since the core software itself remains permanently free under its open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Roundcube is an open-source, PHP-based webmail client that gives any IMAP mail server a modern, AJAX-driven browser interface. First released as a stable version in March 2008 after development led by founder Thomas Bruederli, it has since become one of the most widely deployed self-hosted webmail front ends, bundled by default with hosting control panels such as cPanel and Plesk.

The project is licensed under GNU GPL v3 or later and has been fiscally and organizationally hosted under Nextcloud since 2023, following an earlier attempt at a full rewrite (Roundcube Next) with Kolab Systems that was funded via crowdfunding in 2015 but halted in 2016.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Roundcube supports full IMAP functionality including folder management, message search, spell checking, and MIME-compliant attachment handling, along with an integrated address book and a skinnable, desktop-like interface built on Ajax.

A documented plugin API allows administrators to extend Roundcube with features like calendaring, two-factor authentication, and custom branding, and the project bundles other open-source libraries such as TinyMCE for rich text editing and an HTML5-PHP sanitizer for security.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Roundcube itself is completely free and open source under the GNU GPL; there is no official paid tier, subscription, or per-seat license fee for the core software.

Organizations typically incur only hosting infrastructure costs, since Roundcube runs on a standard LAMP/LEMP stack; some managed hosting providers and hosting panels bundle Roundcube automatically as part of paid hosting plans, but that cost is for the hosting itself, not for a Roundcube license.

features.title: Full IMAP support
features.description: Complete IMAP protocol support for reading, organizing, and searching mail across folders on any standards-compliant mail server.

features.title: Integrated address book
features.description: A built-in contacts manager for storing and organizing email addresses, synced alongside the webmail interface.

features.title: Skinnable, Ajax-driven interface
features.description: A desktop-like browser UI built with Ajax that can be restyled with custom skins for different branding or usability needs.

features.title: Plugin API and extensibility
features.description: A documented plugin architecture that lets administrators add calendaring, two-factor authentication, spam controls, and other features.

features.title: MIME and attachment handling
features.description: Full MIME support for viewing and sending attachments and multi-part messages reliably across mail clients.

features.title: Spell checking
features.description: Built-in spell checking powered by the Googiespell library, integrated directly into the message composer.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs on a standard LAMP/LEMP stack with no proprietary dependencies, giving organizations full control over their mail infrastructure.

features.title: Free, open-source licensing
features.description: Released under GNU GPL v3 or later, allowing free use, modification, and redistribution, with exceptions for skins and plugins.

pros: Completely free and open source with no license fees
No vendor lock-in; runs on an organization's own IMAP/SMTP mail server
Widely supported and bundled by major hosting control panels like cPanel and Plesk
Extensible through a mature plugin ecosystem
Long track record of active development since 2008
cons: Requires self-hosting and server administration knowledge unless bundled by a host
Interface is more utilitarian than modern commercial webmail clients
No official vendor support; help typically comes from community forums or third-party contractors
Advanced features like calendaring require installing separate plugins

faqs.question: Is Roundcube free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Roundcube is free and open source under the GNU GPL v3 or later, with no license fees for the core software.

faqs.question: Who created Roundcube?
faqs.answer: Roundcube was created by Thomas Bruederli, with the first stable release announced in March 2008 after about two years of development.

faqs.question: Is Roundcube a hosted email service?
faqs.answer: No. Roundcube is a webmail client, not a mail hosting provider; it needs to be installed on a server and connected to an existing IMAP/SMTP mail server.

faqs.question: What happened to Roundcube Next?
faqs.answer: Roundcube Next was a planned full rewrite announced with Kolab Systems in 2015 and funded via a crowdfunding campaign that raised over $103,000, but the rewrite effort was halted in 2016.

faqs.question: Who maintains Roundcube today?
faqs.answer: Since 2023, the Roundcube project's organizational and fiscal home has been hosted under Nextcloud, alongside its original community of contributors.

faqs.question: Can Roundcube be extended with plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, Roundcube has a documented plugin API that allows features like calendaring, two-factor authentication, and custom branding to be added.

faqs.question: What server stack does Roundcube require?
faqs.answer: Roundcube runs on a standard LAMP or LEMP stack: Linux, a web server like Apache or Nginx, a database such as MySQL or PostgreSQL, and PHP.

faqs.question: Why do many hosting providers include Roundcube?
faqs.answer: Because it is free, self-hosted, and lightweight, hosting panels such as cPanel adopted Roundcube as a bundled webmail option, replacing older clients like SquirrelMail.

use_cases.title: Shared hosting webmail
use_cases.description: Web hosting providers bundle Roundcube as the default browser-based email client for customers using cPanel, Plesk, or similar control panels.
use_cases.audience: Web hosting companies and their customers

use_cases.title: Self-hosted organizational email
use_cases.description: Businesses, universities, and ISPs running their own IMAP mail servers deploy Roundcube as a free front end instead of paying for a commercial webmail suite.
use_cases.audience: IT administrators at organizations running their own mail servers

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused email setups
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals and organizations pair Roundcube with self-hosted mail servers to avoid third-party webmail providers entirely.

features: GNU GPL v3 or later license

enrichment.short_description: Routine is an AI-powered productivity app that combines tasks, calendar, and notes into one workspace for individuals and teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with individual and team tiers billed monthly
enrichment.company_size: Small startup team, reported around 8 employees
enrichment.seo_title: Routine Review 2026: Pricing, Features and AI Task and Calendar Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Routine's task, calendar, and notes workspace, its AI features, and pricing plans from free to Professional and Business tiers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Routine is a productivity application that merges task management, calendar scheduling, and note-taking into a single workspace, positioning itself against tools like Notion, Todoist, and standalone calendar apps. It was founded in 2020 in Paris, France by Quentin Hocquet and Julien Quintard, went through Y Combinator's Winter 2021 batch, and raised a $2.7 million pre-seed round backed by BoxGroup and Target Global along with angel investors including Clément Delangue and Ian Hogarth; company profile data lists the team at roughly eight employees. The product's interface is organized around an Inbox for quick capture of unplanned tasks, notes, and meeting invitations, an Agenda view for the current day, a Calendar that syncs with Google Calendar (with Outlook and iCloud support added over time), a structured Tasks list split into Unplanned, Recurring, and Upcoming sections, and a dedicated Notes space. Routine has increasingly emphasized AI features, including an AI assistant, AI voice commands, AI-generated meeting notes, and AI automations that act as agents within the workspace, consuming a monthly allotment of 'AI credits' that scales with plan tier. Routine is available on macOS, Windows, the web, and iOS, with Android support in progress as of its most recent public roadmap. Pricing follows a freemium model: the Free plan includes core tasks, calendar, and notes functionality, an AI assistant, offline mode, three integrations, three custom databases, and 250 AI credits per month at no cost. The Personal plan costs $5 per month and adds time blocking, time boxing, exporting, unlimited file uploads, ten integrations, ten custom databases, and 500 AI credits. The Professional plan costs $10 per month and adds AI meeting notes, AI agents, AI automations, a menu bar widget, time tracking, contextual capture, unlimited integrations and custom databases, and 7,000 AI credits. For teams, the Business plan is priced at $15 per seat per month and layers on organization-wide workspaces, access control, versioning, consolidated billing, priority support, and 10,000 AI credits per seat, while an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with unlimited history, compliance controls, audit logs, analytics, user provisioning, and a dedicated success manager. All paid individual and Business plans can be canceled at any time, and Routine markets itself primarily to knowledge workers, freelancers, and small teams who want to consolidate calendar, task, and note tools that would otherwise require separate subscriptions to apps like Google Calendar, Todoist, and Notion.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Routine is a Paris-based productivity app, founded in 2020 by Quentin Hocquet and Julien Quintard and graduated from Y Combinator's W21 batch, that combines calendar, task management, and note-taking into a single AI-assisted workspace rather than requiring users to juggle separate apps.

The product syncs with external calendars such as Google Calendar and organizes work around an Inbox, Agenda, Calendar, Tasks, and Notes structure, with AI features layered on top for meeting notes, automations, and voice commands.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include a quick-capture Inbox, a daily Agenda view, two-way calendar sync, a structured task list separating unplanned, recurring, and upcoming items, and a dedicated notes workspace, all synced across macOS, Windows, web, and iOS.

Paid tiers add AI meeting notes, AI agents and automations, time tracking, time blocking and boxing, unlimited integrations and custom databases, and, for teams, workspace-level access control and consolidated billing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Routine offers a free-forever individual plan, then paid individual tiers at $5/month (Personal) and $10/month (Professional), each unlocking more integrations, AI credits, and automation features, all cancelable at any time.
Team pricing starts at $15 per seat per month for the Business plan, with a custom-quoted Enterprise plan for larger organizations needing compliance, audit logging, and dedicated account management.

features.description: Tools for scheduling focused work blocks and tracking actual time spent on tasks, available from the Personal and Professional tiers respectively.

features.title: Custom databases
features.description: Configurable custom databases for structuring information beyond standard tasks and notes, with limits that expand by plan tier.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Native apps for macOS, Windows, web, and iOS keep tasks, calendar events, and notes synchronized across devices.

features.title: Team workspaces and access control
features.description: Business-tier features including shared organization workspaces, permissioned access control, versioning, and consolidated billing for teams.

Generous free tier includes AI assistant features and multiple integrations
Y Combinator-backed startup with active development and funding
Cross-platform coverage across macOS, Windows, web, and iOS
Transparent, relatively low-cost individual pricing tiers
cons: Android app support has lagged behind other platforms
AI features are metered by monthly credits that can run out on lower tiers
Smaller company size than legacy competitors like Notion or Todoist, which may affect long-term support and roadmap pace

faqs.question: Is Routine free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Routine offers a free plan for individuals that includes tasks, calendar, notes, a basic AI assistant, three integrations, and 250 monthly AI credits.

faqs.question: How much does Routine cost for individuals?
faqs.answer: Paid individual plans are Personal at $5/month and Professional at $10/month, both billed monthly and cancelable anytime.

faqs.question: Does Routine have a team plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Business plan costs $15 per seat per month and adds shared workspaces, access control, and consolidated billing; an Enterprise plan with custom pricing is also available.

faqs.question: Who founded Routine?
faqs.answer: Routine was founded in 2020 in Paris, France by Quentin Hocquet and Julien Quintard, and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2021 batch.

faqs.question: What calendars does Routine sync with?
faqs.answer: Routine integrates with Google Calendar natively, with support for Microsoft Outlook and iCloud Calendar also available.

faqs.question: Does Routine work on Android?
faqs.answer: Routine has historically prioritized macOS, Windows, web, and iOS, with Android support noted as in-progress in the company's public roadmap.

faqs.question: What are Routine's AI credits?
faqs.answer: AI credits are a monthly usage allotment that powers Routine's AI assistant, meeting notes, and automation features; the allotment scales up with each paid plan tier.

faqs.question: Has Routine raised funding?
faqs.answer: Yes, Routine raised a $2.7 million pre-seed round from investors including BoxGroup and Target Global, alongside participation from several angel investors.

features: AI assistant and AI voice commands

enrichment.short_description: Routr is a free, open-source SIP proxy, registrar, and location server built by Fonoster for deploying VoIP and telephony infrastructure on Docker and Kubernetes.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, self-hosted; no official paid SaaS tier for Routr itself
enrichment.seo_title: Routr (routr.io): Open-Source SIP Server for VoIP Infrastructure
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Routr is, how this open-source SIP proxy, registrar, and location server from Fonoster works, and how it deploys on Docker and Kubernetes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Routr, found at routr.io, is a lightweight, open-source SIP (Session Initiation Protocol) server that functions as a proxy, location server, and registrar for building reliable, scalable VoIP and telephony infrastructure. It is built and maintained by Fonoster, a company also known for its open-source alternative to Twilio's communications APIs, and the Routr codebase is publicly available on GitHub under the fonoster/routr repository, described there as 'the future of programmable SIP servers.' Routr is explicitly cloud-native: it is designed to run on Docker and Kubernetes (installable via Helm charts), as well as directly on major operating systems, so telephony carriers, communication service providers, and integrators can deploy SIP infrastructure without the operational complexity or licensing cost typically associated with traditional carrier-grade SIP servers. The server can be controlled through multiple interfaces, including a command-line tool for remote server management, a RESTful API for programmatic control, and a web-based GUI, which gives it flexibility for both manual administration and automated infrastructure-as-code workflows. Architecturally, Routr implements the standard SIP server roles of Proxy, Registrar, and Location Service, and supports configurable routing strategies such as Intra-Domain, Domain Ingress, Domain Egress, and Peer Egress routing, along with multi-tenant and multi-domain support backed by domain-level access control lists. Its modular design allows developers to extend functionality by writing custom Processors and Middleware, rather than being limited to the server's built-in behavior, which makes it suitable for teams that need to customize call routing logic for specific carrier or enterprise requirements. Because Routr is open source, it can be inspected, modified, and self-hosted freely, with no licensing fee for the software itself; organizations instead bear only their own infrastructure and, if desired, support costs. Routr is best understood as developer- and infrastructure-focused tooling rather than a consumer-facing product: its target users are telephony engineers, VoIP integrators, and communication service providers building or operating their own SIP-based calling infrastructure, often as a self-hosted alternative or complement to commercial SIP trunking and session border controller products. Given its open-source, community-driven nature, detailed corporate facts such as Fonoster's exact founding year, headquarters city, and team size are inconsistently reported across third-party company databases and are not confirmed here to avoid stating unverified figures.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Routr is an open-source SIP proxy, registrar, and location server built by Fonoster for teams that need to run their own VoIP or telephony infrastructure. It is designed to be cloud-native, running on Docker and Kubernetes as well as traditional operating systems, and is published as open-source code on GitHub under fonoster/routr.

The project targets telephony carriers, communication service providers, and integrators who want a lightweight, programmable SIP server instead of a heavyweight commercial session border controller or SIP trunking platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Routr implements the core SIP server functions of Proxy, Registrar, and Location Service, with configurable routing strategies for intra-domain, domain ingress, domain egress, and peer egress traffic, plus multi-tenant support with domain-level access control lists.

It can be controlled via a command-line interface, a RESTful API, or a web-based GUI, and its modular architecture allows developers to extend behavior with custom Processors and Middleware rather than being confined to built-in functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Routr is free and open source; there is no licensing fee to download, self-host, and modify the software. Costs to an organization are limited to the infrastructure used to run it, such as cloud compute for Docker or Kubernetes deployments.

There is no separate commercial SaaS tier of Routr documented on routr.io; organizations needing managed communications infrastructure would instead look at Fonoster's broader open-source communications platform.

features.title: SIP proxy, registrar, and location server
features.description: Implements the three core SIP server roles needed to route, register, and locate endpoints in a VoIP network.

features.title: Docker and Kubernetes native
features.description: Ships as a Docker image and can be deployed to Kubernetes via Helm, designed for cloud-native infrastructure from the ground up.

features.title: RESTful API and CLI control
features.description: Supports remote administration through a command-line tool, a RESTful API, or a web-based GUI for teams that prefer point-and-click management.

features.title: Configurable routing strategies
features.description: Supports Intra-Domain, Domain Ingress, Domain Egress, and Peer Egress routing patterns for flexible call routing logic.

features.title: Multi-tenant, multi-domain support
features.description: Domain-level access control lists allow a single Routr deployment to serve multiple tenants or customer domains securely.

features.title: Extensible via Processors and Middleware
features.description: A modular plugin-style architecture lets developers write custom Processors and Middleware to extend or override default server behavior.

features.title: Open-source and self-hosted
features.description: Fully open-source codebase available on GitHub, allowing organizations to inspect, modify, and self-host without licensing fees.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing cost for the software
Cloud-native design that deploys cleanly on Docker and Kubernetes
Multiple control interfaces (CLI, REST API, GUI) suit different operational preferences
Extensible architecture for teams with custom SIP routing requirements
Built by Fonoster, a team focused on open-source communications infrastructure
cons: Requires SIP and telephony infrastructure expertise to deploy and operate safely
No official managed/hosted SaaS version documented, so operations burden falls on the deploying team
Smaller community and ecosystem compared to established commercial SBC and SIP server vendors
Corporate details about the maintaining company are not consistently documented publicly

faqs.question: What is Routr used for?
faqs.answer: Routr is used to build and operate SIP-based VoIP infrastructure, acting as a proxy, registrar, and location server for telephony carriers, service providers, and integrators.

faqs.question: Is Routr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Routr is free and open source; there is no license fee for the software itself, only the cost of the infrastructure used to run it.

faqs.question: Who develops Routr?
faqs.answer: Routr is developed by Fonoster, a team also known for building an open-source alternative to Twilio's communications APIs.

faqs.question: Does Routr run on Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, Routr is designed to be cloud-native and can be deployed to Kubernetes using Helm, in addition to running on Docker or directly on major operating systems.

faqs.question: How is Routr different from a router hardware product?
faqs.answer: Routr is unrelated to networking router hardware; it is software that performs SIP proxy, registrar, and location-server functions for VoIP call routing, not physical network traffic routing.

faqs.question: Can Routr be controlled programmatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, Routr exposes a RESTful API for programmatic control in addition to a command-line interface and a web-based GUI.

faqs.question: Is Routr suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Routr assumes familiarity with SIP and telephony concepts; it is aimed at engineers and integrators building VoIP infrastructure rather than non-technical users.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted VoIP carrier infrastructure
use_cases.description: Telephony carriers and communication service providers deploy Routr as their SIP proxy and registrar to avoid the cost of commercial session border controllers.
use_cases.audience: Telephony carriers and communication service providers

use_cases.title: Cloud-native SIP deployments
use_cases.description: Engineering teams deploy Routr on Kubernetes or Docker to run scalable, containerized SIP infrastructure alongside other cloud-native services.

use_cases.title: Custom call routing logic
use_cases.description: Integrators extend Routr with custom Processors and Middleware to implement specialized routing rules for enterprise or multi-tenant SIP deployments.

features: SIP proxy, registrar, and location server
RESTful API, CLI, and web GUI control
Extensible via custom Processors and Middleware

enrichment.short_description: RSS.com is a paid podcast hosting and monetization platform offering unlimited storage, distribution, transcripts, and analytics for podcasters.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with a free tier and paid annual-billed plans
enrichment.seo_title: RSS.com Review 2026: Podcast Hosting Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare RSS.com's free and paid podcast hosting plans, monetization tools, and distribution features for podcasters in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RSS.com is a podcast hosting, distribution, and monetization platform that positions itself as an affordable, all-in-one alternative to hosts like Buzzsprout, Libsyn, and Anchor. While the RSS.com domain has existed since the mid-2000s, the company built out its current podcast-hosting product and launched the platform in its present form around 2018, and has continued expanding features since. It is based in the United States and reported around 50 employees and roughly $5.5 million in annual revenue as of 2025, according to third-party company-data sources. RSS.com's core offering includes unlimited podcast episodes and unlimited audio storage even on its free plan, called Local and Niche, which also includes a public RSS feed, an embeddable player, a podcast website builder, episode scheduling, basic analytics, automatic distribution to major directories such as Spotify, Apple Podcasts, and Amazon Music, listener-donation and Value-for-Value monetization tools, copyright protection, and human customer support, all at no cost. Paid plans unlock monetization and team features: the All in One Podcasting plan is priced at $11.99 per month when billed annually and adds dynamic ad insertion monetization available from as few as 10 downloads per episode, Apple Podcasts Subscriptions earnings, audio-to-video conversion for YouTube, team member invitations, an unbranded player, AI-generated transcripts, advanced analytics, access to an exclusive Slack community, and priority support. The top-tier Podcast Networks plan is priced at $18.75 per month when billed annually and adds support for unlimited podcasts and team seats, unlimited podcast-level analytics, API access, automatic transcripts across shows, and unbranded YouTube video exports, aimed at podcast networks managing multiple shows under one account. RSS.com differentiates itself from many competitors by offering unlimited episodes and storage even on its free tier, whereas several rival hosts cap free-tier episode counts or storage size, and by bundling monetization tools like dynamic ad insertion and Value-for-Value/listener-donation support directly into its plans rather than as a separate add-on product. The platform is aimed primarily at independent podcasters, hobbyist and niche show creators who want a genuinely free starting point, and growing shows or small podcast networks that need multi-show management, team collaboration, and monetization once they are ready to scale beyond a single free-tier show.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RSS.com is a podcast hosting and monetization platform built to let creators launch a show, distribute it to major podcast directories, and eventually monetize it through ads, subscriptions, and listener donations, all from one dashboard. The current product was built out and launched around 2018, and the company is based in the United States with a team of roughly 50 people as of recent reporting.
The platform is positioned as a budget-friendly alternative to established podcast hosts, most notably by offering unlimited episodes and unlimited audio storage even on its permanently free plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: All RSS.com plans, including the free tier, include unlimited episodes, unlimited storage, automatic distribution to directories like Spotify and Apple Podcasts, a podcast website, an embeddable player, and episode scheduling.

Paid plans add monetization tools such as dynamic ad insertion from as few as 10 downloads, Apple Podcasts Subscriptions support, listener donations, AI-generated transcripts, audio-to-video YouTube conversion, and advanced or unlimited analytics depending on tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free Local and Niche plan requires no credit card and includes unlimited episodes and storage plus basic distribution and analytics. The All in One Podcasting plan costs $11.99/month billed annually and adds monetization and team features.

The top Podcast Networks plan costs $18.75/month billed annually and is built for managing unlimited podcasts and team seats with unlimited analytics and API access, targeting podcast networks rather than single-show creators.

features.title: Unlimited episodes and storage
features.description: Every plan, including the free tier, allows unlimited podcast episodes and unlimited audio storage with no length caps.

features.title: Automatic directory distribution
features.description: Podcasts are automatically distributed to major platforms including Spotify, Apple Podcasts, and Amazon Music from a single RSS feed.

features.title: Dynamic ad insertion monetization
features.description: Paid plans let shows monetize with dynamic ad insertion starting from as few as 10 downloads per episode.

features.title: AI-generated transcripts
features.description: Automatic AI-powered episode transcripts are available on paid plans, useful for accessibility and SEO.

features.title: Podcast website and embeddable player
features.description: Every plan includes an auto-generated podcast website and a customizable embeddable player for sharing episodes.

features.title: Value for Value and listener donations
features.description: Built-in support for listener donations and Value-for-Value monetization models, available even on the free plan.

features.title: Audio-to-video YouTube conversion
features.description: Paid plans can automatically convert audio episodes into video files formatted for YouTube distribution.

features.title: Team and multi-show management
features.description: The Podcast Networks plan supports unlimited shows and team seats with API access for managing a portfolio of podcasts.

pros: Unlimited episodes and storage available even on the permanently free plan
Built-in monetization tools like dynamic ad insertion and listener donations
Automatic distribution to all major podcast directories
Affordable annual pricing compared to several competitors
Includes AI transcripts and audio-to-video conversion on paid tiers
cons: Lower-cost pricing shown on the site is tied to annual billing, so monthly billing costs more
Advanced monetization features are locked behind paid plans
Smaller company than legacy podcast hosts, which may mean a smaller support and integration ecosystem
Podcast Networks-level features like API access require the top-tier plan

faqs.question: Is RSS.com free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RSS.com offers a permanently free Local and Niche plan with unlimited episodes and storage, no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does RSS.com cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $11.99/month for the All in One Podcasting plan when billed annually, with the Podcast Networks plan at $18.75/month billed annually.

faqs.question: Does RSS.com support podcast monetization?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include dynamic ad insertion available from as few as 10 downloads per episode, Apple Podcasts Subscriptions support, and Value-for-Value listener donations.

faqs.question: Does RSS.com distribute to Spotify and Apple Podcasts?
faqs.answer: Yes, all plans include automatic distribution to major podcast directories including Spotify, Apple Podcasts, and Amazon Music.

faqs.question: Can RSS.com convert podcasts to video?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include audio-to-video conversion for publishing episodes as YouTube videos.

faqs.question: Does RSS.com offer transcripts?
faqs.answer: Yes, AI-generated transcripts are available on paid plans, with automatic transcripts included at the Podcast Networks tier.

faqs.question: Is RSS.com good for podcast networks?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Podcast Networks plan supports unlimited shows, unlimited team seats, unlimited analytics, and API access for managing multiple shows.

faqs.question: When was RSS.com launched?
faqs.answer: The RSS.com domain existed earlier, but the company built out and launched its current podcast hosting platform around 2018.

enrichment.short_description: RSSHub is a free, open-source RSS feed generator that creates RSS feeds from thousands of websites and services that do not natively offer one.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (AGPL-3.0 license); self-hostable, with free public community instances also available
enrichment.seo_title: RSSHub: Free Open-Source RSS Feed Generator for Any Website
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what RSSHub is, how this open-source RSS feed generator with 1000+ routes and 5000+ instances works, and how to self-host or use public feeds.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RSSHub is a free, open-source RSS feed generator created and maintained by developer DIYgod, designed to make 'everything RSSible' by producing RSS feeds for websites, apps, and services that do not natively publish one. Released under the AGPL-3.0 license, the project's source code is hosted on GitHub at DIYgod/RSSHub and has accumulated more than 45,000 GitHub stars, reflecting its scale as one of the largest and most actively maintained open-source RSS tools. RSSHub describes itself as the world's largest RSS network, made up of more than 5,000 public instances run by community members, and its route library, built by more than 900 contributors and maintainers, covers over 1,000 pre-built routes spanning social media platforms, news sites, e-commerce listings, video platforms, forums, and many other categories of web content that would otherwise require manual checking. Because RSS feed support has declined across the modern web as many platforms drop native RSS in favor of closed apps and algorithmic feeds, RSSHub fills that gap by scraping or querying a source through code-defined 'routes' and outputting a standards-compliant RSS or Atom feed that any feed reader can subscribe to. Users can either run their own self-hosted RSSHub instance, using the official Docker image or npm package for full control and reliability, or use one of the many free public instances maintained by community members, trading some reliability and rate limits for zero setup effort. The project's ecosystem includes companion tools such as RSSHub Radar, a browser extension that detects RSS and RSSHub-compatible feeds on the page a user is currently viewing and offers one-click subscription, along with mobile counterparts RSSBud for iOS and RSSAid for Android built with Flutter. Because RSSHub is entirely open source and community-driven, there is no company behind it, no headquarters, and no official employee count; contributions and route maintenance come from a distributed volunteer community coordinated primarily through GitHub. RSSHub is popular among developers, researchers, journalists, and power users of RSS readers who want to track updates from sources such as social media accounts, government notices, price trackers, or niche blogs that lack an official feed, and it is frequently paired with self-hosted RSS reader software as part of a broader self-hosted or privacy-focused content-tracking stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RSSHub is an open-source project created by developer DIYgod that generates RSS feeds for websites and services that do not offer one natively, addressing the broader decline of native RSS support across the modern web.

The project is released under the AGPL-3.0 license, hosted at github.com/DIYgod/RSSHub, and has grown into what its own documentation describes as the world's largest RSS network, with over 5,000 public community-run instances and more than 1,000 pre-built content routes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RSSHub's core feature is its library of over 1,000 routes, each a small piece of code that scrapes or queries a specific website or service and outputs the result as a standards-compliant RSS feed that any feed reader can subscribe to.

The ecosystem also includes RSSHub Radar, a browser extension for auto-detecting subscribable feeds on the current page, plus mobile companion apps RSSBud (iOS) and RSSAid (Android) for discovering and managing RSSHub-generated feeds on mobile.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RSSHub is entirely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license; there is no paid tier, subscription, or commercial edition of the software itself.

Users can self-host RSSHub at no software cost beyond their own server infrastructure, or use one of the many free public instances run by community members, which trade guaranteed uptime for zero setup effort.

features.title: 1,000-plus pre-built content routes
features.description: A large library of code-defined routes that generate RSS feeds from social media, news, e-commerce, video, and other web sources lacking native feeds.

features.title: Self-hosting via Docker or npm
features.description: RSSHub can be deployed on a user's own server using the official Docker image or npm package for full control over uptime and rate limits.

features.title: Free public community instances
features.description: Thousands of publicly run RSSHub instances let users subscribe to generated feeds without hosting anything themselves.

features.title: RSSHub Radar browser extension
features.description: A companion browser extension that automatically detects RSS and RSSHub-compatible feeds on the page a user is viewing for one-click subscription.

features.title: Mobile companion apps
features.description: RSSBud for iOS and RSSAid for Android extend RSSHub Radar's feed-discovery functionality to mobile devices.

features.title: Open, community-driven route contributions
features.description: Over 900 contributors maintain and expand the route library, with a documented process for adding new source routes.

features.title: AGPL-3.0 open-source license
features.description: The full source code is publicly available and licensed under AGPL-3.0, allowing inspection, modification, and self-hosted deployment.

pros: Completely free with no paid tier or usage fees
Massive route library covering over 1,000 distinct content sources
Active, large open-source community with 45,000-plus GitHub stars
Flexible deployment via self-hosting or free public instances
Companion browser extension and mobile apps simplify feed discovery
cons: No official company or support contract; help comes from community channels
Public instances can be rate-limited or unreliable compared to self-hosting
Self-hosting requires server administration and Docker or Node.js familiarity
Some routes can break when source websites change their structure, requiring community fixes

faqs.question: Is RSSHub free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RSSHub is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with no paid tier.

faqs.question: Who created RSSHub?
faqs.answer: RSSHub was created and is primarily maintained by developer DIYgod, with contributions from a community of more than 900 contributors.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host RSSHub?
faqs.answer: No, RSSHub can be used via one of thousands of free public community instances, though self-hosting via Docker or npm gives more control and reliability.

faqs.question: How many sources does RSSHub support?
faqs.answer: RSSHub includes more than 1,000 pre-built routes covering social media, news, e-commerce, video platforms, and many other categories of websites.

faqs.question: What license does RSSHub use?
faqs.answer: RSSHub is released under the AGPL-3.0 open-source license.

faqs.question: Is there a browser extension for RSSHub?
faqs.answer: Yes, RSSHub Radar is a browser extension that detects RSS and RSSHub-compatible feeds on the current page for one-click subscription.

faqs.question: Is RSSHub different from RSS.com?
faqs.answer: Yes, RSSHub is a free open-source RSS feed generator for sites without native feeds, while RSS.com is an unrelated paid podcast hosting company; they share no ownership or history.

faqs.question: How large is the RSSHub community?
faqs.answer: RSSHub describes itself as the world's largest RSS network with over 5,000 public instances and has more than 45,000 stars on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Tracking sources without native RSS
use_cases.description: Developers and researchers use RSSHub to generate feeds for social media accounts, government notices, or niche sites that never offered RSS.
use_cases.audience: Developers, researchers, and power users of RSS readers

use_cases.title: Self-hosted content monitoring stacks
use_cases.description: Privacy-focused users self-host RSSHub alongside a self-hosted RSS reader to build a fully independent content-tracking pipeline.

use_cases.title: Journalism and OSINT source monitoring
use_cases.description: Journalists and researchers use RSSHub routes to monitor updates from social accounts, forums, and listings relevant to ongoing reporting or investigations.

features: Access to thousands of free public instances

enrichment.short_description: RudderStack is a warehouse-native customer data platform that collects, unifies, and routes customer event data to a company's own data warehouse and downstream tools.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based subscription with a free tier based on monthly event volume, plus Growth and custom Enterprise plans
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 130 employees (reported range of 127 to 150 across sources)
enrichment.seo_title: RudderStack Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Warehouse-Native CDP
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how RudderStack's warehouse-native customer data platform works, its event volume-based pricing tiers, and its core data collection and activation features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RudderStack is a warehouse-native customer data platform (CDP) founded in July 2019 by CEO Soumyadeb Mitra and Executive Chairman Shvetank Jain, headquartered in San Francisco, California. Unlike traditional CDPs that store customer data in a proprietary system, RudderStack is built around the idea of running data collection, identity resolution, and activation directly on top of a customer's own data warehouse, such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, or Redshift, so companies retain ownership and control of their data rather than duplicating it into a vendor-hosted store. The platform originated as an open-source project and remains open source at its core, alongside a hosted commercial offering, giving engineering teams the option to self-host or use RudderStack's managed cloud service. Its core capabilities span three areas: event collection through more than a dozen SDKs across web, mobile, and server environments; routing of that event data to more than 200 supported cloud destinations, such as marketing, analytics, and advertising tools; and reverse ETL, which activates data already modeled in the warehouse by syncing it back out to operational and go-to-market tools. RudderStack's pricing is usage-based and tied to monthly event volume. The Free plan supports up to 250,000 events per month and includes access to 16 SDK sources, 200-plus cloud destinations, warehouse destinations, reverse ETL, one tracking plan, a five-transformation limit, up to 10 team members, and a single production workspace, all at no cost. The Growth plan starts at $265 per month for up to 1 million events (scalable to 25 million-plus with added spend), offers a 15 percent discount for annual commitments, and adds unlimited team members, unlimited tracking plans, 30-minute warehouse sync intervals, two workspaces, 25 reverse ETL connections, a 25-transformation limit, advanced consent management integration, dedicated Slack support, and up to six months of onboarding assistance. The custom-priced Enterprise plan is aimed at large organizations and includes everything in Growth plus 5-minute warehouse sync, unlimited transformations, access to RudderStack's Profiles and Data Apps features, HIPAA compliance support, single sign-on, VPC deployment options, and white-glove support with a dedicated Technical Account Manager, Customer Success Manager, and Solutions Architect. Company-size estimates place RudderStack at roughly 127 to 150 employees as of 2026, spread across North America, Europe, and Asia. RudderStack is most commonly adopted by data engineering and analytics teams at mid-market and enterprise companies that want first-party, warehouse-native customer data infrastructure as a more controllable and cost-predictable alternative to legacy CDPs like Segment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RudderStack is a warehouse-native customer data platform founded in July 2019 by Soumyadeb Mitra and Shvetank Jain and headquartered in San Francisco. Its defining architectural choice is running data collection, identity resolution, and activation directly on a customer's own warehouse, such as Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, or Redshift, rather than duplicating data into a proprietary vendor-hosted store.

The platform has open-source roots and continues to offer both a self-hosted open-source option and a fully managed commercial cloud service, appealing to engineering-led teams that want more control over their customer data infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include event collection via 16-plus SDKs across web, mobile, and server platforms, routing to more than 200 cloud destinations, and reverse ETL for syncing warehouse-modeled data back out to operational tools.

Higher tiers add faster warehouse sync intervals, unlimited data transformations, identity resolution features under RudderStack's Profiles product, and enterprise security options including HIPAA compliance, SSO, and VPC deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RudderStack's Free plan covers up to 250,000 events per month with access to core sources, destinations, and reverse ETL at no cost. The Growth plan starts at $265/month for up to 1 million events, with a 15 percent discount for annual billing and scalability to 25 million-plus events.

The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds 5-minute warehouse sync, unlimited transformations, HIPAA compliance, SSO, VPC deployment, and dedicated technical and customer success support for large organizations.

features.title: Warehouse-native architecture
features.description: Runs data collection, identity resolution, and activation directly on the customer's own warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, Redshift) instead of a proprietary data store.

features.title: 16-plus SDK sources
features.description: Event collection SDKs across web, mobile, and server-side environments for capturing first-party customer data.

features.title: 200-plus cloud destinations

features.title: Reverse ETL
features.description: Syncs data already modeled in the warehouse back out to operational and go-to-market tools, activating warehouse data for business teams.

features.title: Configurable data transformations
features.description: Lets teams clean, filter, or enrich event data in transit, with transformation limits that expand by plan tier.

features.title: Tracking plans
features.description: Schema governance tooling that lets teams define and enforce expected event structures across sources.

features.title: Enterprise security and compliance
features.description: HIPAA compliance, single sign-on, and VPC deployment options available on the Enterprise plan for regulated or security-sensitive organizations.

features.title: Open-source core
features.description: RudderStack's core technology has open-source roots, giving engineering teams the option to self-host rather than use the managed cloud service exclusively.

pros: Warehouse-native model keeps first-party customer data under the company's own control
Generous free tier covering up to 250,000 events per month
Open-source option available for teams that prefer self-hosting
Wide destination coverage with over 200 integrations
Reverse ETL is built in rather than requiring a separate tool
cons: Pricing scales with event volume, which can grow costly at high traffic levels
Enterprise-grade features like fast warehouse sync and SSO require custom Enterprise pricing
Warehouse-native model assumes the customer already has a modern cloud data warehouse in place
Smaller company size than legacy CDP competitors, which may affect support scale

faqs.question: Is RudderStack free?
faqs.answer: Yes, RudderStack offers a free plan supporting up to 250,000 events per month with access to core sources, destinations, and reverse ETL.

faqs.question: How much does RudderStack cost?
faqs.answer: The Growth plan starts at $265/month for up to 1 million events, scalable to 25 million or more, with a 15 percent discount for annual billing; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Is RudderStack open source?
faqs.answer: RudderStack has open-source roots and offers a self-hosted option alongside its managed commercial cloud service.

faqs.question: What is warehouse-native customer data platform architecture?
faqs.answer: It means collection, identity resolution, and activation run directly on the customer's own data warehouse, such as Snowflake or BigQuery, instead of copying data into a separate vendor-owned store.

faqs.question: Who founded RudderStack?
faqs.answer: RudderStack was founded in July 2019 by CEO Soumyadeb Mitra and Executive Chairman Shvetank Jain, and is headquartered in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Does RudderStack support reverse ETL?
faqs.answer: Yes, reverse ETL is a built-in feature that syncs warehouse-modeled data back out to operational and marketing tools.

faqs.question: How many destinations does RudderStack integrate with?
faqs.answer: RudderStack supports more than 200 cloud destinations for routing collected customer event data.

faqs.question: Is RudderStack HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: HIPAA compliance support is available on RudderStack's custom-priced Enterprise plan.

White-glove support with TAM, CSM, and Solutions Architect

enrichment.short_description: Rundeck is runbook automation and job scheduling software for operations teams, now owned by PagerDuty as part of its Process Automation product line.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open-core: free open-source Community Edition, with a commercially licensed Enterprise/Process Automation tier priced via custom quote
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); Enterprise pricing custom, industry-reported to average around $51,000/year
enrichment.company_size: Operated as part of PagerDuty, Inc. (NYSE: PD), a public company; standalone Rundeck team size not publicly disclosed
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (part of PagerDuty, Inc.)
enrichment.seo_title: Rundeck Review 2026: Pricing, Features and PagerDuty Ownership Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Rundeck's runbook automation and job scheduling platform works, its free Community Edition versus paid Enterprise tier, and its PagerDuty acquisition.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Rundeck is runbook automation and job scheduling software originally released in 2010 by SimplifyOps, a company formally founded in 2013 by Damon Edwards, Alex Honor, and Greg Schueler, the same people behind the open-source Rundeck project. Rundeck was built to let operations teams automate routine maintenance tasks and delegate controlled access to run specific jobs without granting broad system access, effectively turning manual runbooks into repeatable, auditable, self-service automations. On October 1, 2020, Rundeck was acquired by PagerDuty, the publicly traded incident-response and operations company (NYSE: PD), for approximately $100 million in a mix of cash and stock; PagerDuty has stated that the acquisition did not change pricing for its existing PagerDuty products, and Rundeck continues to be offered as a distinct product line, now marketed as PagerDuty Process Automation. Rundeck's job scheduling system supports both cron-style scheduled jobs and event-driven triggers, and its workflow engine handles multi-step operational processes with conditional logic, error handling, retry mechanisms, and parallel step execution across distributed infrastructure. The product is offered in three tiers: the open-source Community Edition, which is free and includes core workflow execution, community plugins, encryption, scheduling, activity logging, and key storage; a self-hosted Enterprise edition (PagerDuty Process Automation Self-Hosted), which adds high-availability clustering with auto-takeover, single sign-on authentication, certified enterprise plugins for tools like PagerDuty, ServiceNow, and Datadog, enhanced rule-based webhooks, a workflow visualization and configuration GUI, and job queuing and load balancing for scale; and a fully managed SaaS edition (PagerDuty Process Automation), which adds a 99.9 percent uptime SLA managed directly by PagerDuty along with AI-assisted runbook generation and ROI metrics. Neither the self-hosted Enterprise nor the SaaS tier publishes list pricing; both require contacting PagerDuty sales for a custom quote, though third-party industry sources have reported average enterprise contract values around $51,000 per year. Because Rundeck is now a PagerDuty product, standalone company size and revenue figures are not separately disclosed; PagerDuty as a whole is a publicly traded company. Rundeck's core users are IT operations, DevOps, and site reliability engineering teams at organizations, including many Fortune 500 companies, that need to standardize and delegate operational tasks like server restarts, deployments, and incident remediation steps without giving every engineer full production access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rundeck is runbook automation and job scheduling software first released in 2010 by SimplifyOps, a company founded by Damon Edwards, Alex Honor, and Greg Schueler, and acquired by PagerDuty on October 1, 2020 for approximately $100 million in cash and stock.

The product turns manual operational runbooks into repeatable, self-service automations that let operations, DevOps, and SRE teams execute standardized tasks like deployments and remediations without granting broad production access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Rundeck's job scheduling supports cron-style schedules and event-driven triggers, and its workflow engine handles multi-step processes with conditional logic, error handling, retries, and parallel execution across distributed infrastructure.

Enterprise and SaaS tiers add high-availability clustering, single sign-on, certified plugins for tools like ServiceNow and Datadog, a visual workflow builder, job queuing and load balancing, and, more recently, AI-assisted runbook generation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition of Rundeck is free and open source, covering core workflow execution, scheduling, community plugins, and activity logging for teams willing to self-manage reliability and support.

The self-hosted Enterprise tier and the fully managed PagerDuty Process Automation SaaS tier are both custom-priced via PagerDuty sales, with third-party sources reporting average enterprise contract values around $51,000 per year.

features.title: Cron-style and event-driven job scheduling
features.description: Supports both traditional cron-syntax scheduled jobs and event-driven triggers for reactive automation.

features.title: Multi-step workflow engine
features.description: Handles complex operational processes with conditional logic, error handling, retry mechanisms, and parallel step execution.

features.title: Self-service access delegation
features.description: Lets administrators grant specific users or teams the ability to run predefined jobs without full underlying system access.

features.title: Certified enterprise plugins
features.description: Enterprise tier includes certified integrations with tools such as PagerDuty, ServiceNow, and Datadog for connected incident and change workflows.

features.title: High-availability clustering
features.description: Enterprise and SaaS tiers support HA clustering with auto-takeover to keep automation running during node failures.

features.title: Visual workflow builder
features.description: A GUI-based workflow visualization and configuration tool available on the Enterprise and SaaS tiers for building jobs without hand-writing definitions.

features.title: Activity logging and key storage
features.description: Built-in audit logging of job executions and secure key storage for credentials, available even in the free Community Edition.

features.title: AI-assisted runbook generation
features.description: The managed PagerDuty Process Automation SaaS tier includes AI-generated runbook suggestions and ROI metrics for automation initiatives.

pros: Free, open-source Community Edition available for teams that want to self-host at no cost
Backed by PagerDuty's incident-response ecosystem and integrations
Mature product with a long track record dating back to 2010
Supports both self-hosted and fully managed SaaS deployment models
Used by many Fortune 500 companies, indicating enterprise-grade reliability at scale
cons: Enterprise and SaaS pricing is not published and requires contacting sales
Community Edition lacks high availability, SSO, and certified plugins found in paid tiers
Reported average enterprise contract value (~$51,000/year) is a significant cost for smaller teams
Now fully owned by PagerDuty, so its roadmap is tied to PagerDuty's broader product strategy rather than operating independently

faqs.question: Is Rundeck free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rundeck's Community Edition is free and open source, though it lacks enterprise features like high availability, SSO, and certified plugins found in the paid Enterprise and SaaS tiers.

faqs.question: Who owns Rundeck?
faqs.answer: Rundeck is owned by PagerDuty, which acquired it on October 1, 2020 for approximately $100 million in cash and stock; it is now marketed as PagerDuty Process Automation.

faqs.question: How much does Rundeck Enterprise cost?
faqs.answer: Enterprise and SaaS pricing is not publicly listed and requires a custom quote from PagerDuty sales; third-party sources report average enterprise contracts around $51,000 per year.

faqs.question: When was Rundeck founded?
faqs.answer: Rundeck was first released in 2010 by SimplifyOps, a company founded by Damon Edwards, Alex Honor, and Greg Schueler.

faqs.question: What is Rundeck used for?
faqs.answer: Rundeck is used for runbook automation and job scheduling, letting operations and DevOps teams automate and delegate routine tasks like deployments, restarts, and incident remediation.

faqs.question: Does Rundeck support Kubernetes and cloud infrastructure?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rundeck is commonly used to automate tasks across distributed and cloud infrastructure, and integrates with tools such as Datadog and ServiceNow via certified plugins on the Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Rundeck Community and Enterprise?
faqs.answer: Community is free and open source with core scheduling and workflow features, while Enterprise adds high-availability clustering, SSO, certified plugins, a visual workflow builder, and job queuing for scale.

faqs.question: Is there a managed, hosted version of Rundeck?
faqs.answer: Yes, PagerDuty offers a fully managed SaaS edition called PagerDuty Process Automation, which includes a 99.9 percent uptime SLA and AI-assisted runbook generation.

use_cases.title: Automating routine IT operations
use_cases.description: Operations teams use Rundeck to automate repetitive maintenance tasks like server restarts, backups, and deployments on a schedule.

use_cases.title: Delegated, self-service job execution
use_cases.description: Organizations use Rundeck's access controls to let non-admin staff run predefined jobs safely without granting full production system access.

use_cases.title: Incident remediation runbooks
use_cases.description: SRE teams pair Rundeck with PagerDuty's incident response tools to automate remediation steps triggered during an active incident.

features: Certified enterprise plugins (PagerDuty, ServiceNow, Datadog, and more)
